speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about holidays. Tell me about some of the other holidays. What was special about them? [SHRUGS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They were nice. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't like Rosh Hashanah. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't like fasting days. [LAUGHS] Then comes Yom Kippur. I didn't like that. Rosh Hashanah was not bad. Yom Kippur was a very sad holiday. Plus we had to fast, and-- you know-- and pray a lot. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a girl, I didn't-- I was in shul, but, you know, you had to-- you had to pray. And-- what other holidays are there? Like Hanukkah was happy. You get some money.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [SHRUGS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. Tell me about school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I liked school. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was very happy to go to school. What kind of school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- a elementary school. But you were-- you finished eight years, just like high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken in that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakian. And, you know, we had to know Russian, we had to know German. We had twice a week, we had Russian and German-- an hour. And that, you have to do it, and have to pass it. Otherwise you can't pass your grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school was it? Was it assimilated with different religions and with boys and girls, or how was it set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had boys there too. And some of them, they weren't Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship like with the other students?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean? With Christians? I had nothing to do with them. [SHRUGS] There was a lot of-- there were more Jewish-- Jewish Jews then-- because there is-- was also a Russian school there, too. Later on, there were a Hebrew school, Hebrew Gymnasium. But that was later, they built it. But before, we had that kind of a school. Matter of fact, they didn't have that much place, so they used to put four grade and five grade together. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were some of your friends in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends? Other girls. We were good friends, and used to come in my house after school. We used to make together homework, used to have a  bite together. And matter of fact, I had one friend, from school, from Munkacs, she lives now in Miami.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Raji-- Raji Sin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was special about the friendship with you and Raji?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was also with her mother. They were very close friends. And it was like a family. Well, I mean, I had other friends, too. But she who remained, you know-- that she's still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So her-- I had other-- I had friends, and other friends, too. It's just her. I had a lot of friends, matter of fact. As soon as we had time, like in the middle of the week, even, I have time, before Shabbos, we used to get together. We used to go eat ice cream. Not for money.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I mean, you paid the next day. We had fun. That was all in Czechoslovakia. But later, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember any teachers, anyone significant?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember just those teachers that I had. And then also, you had an hour, like-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know how they call it here.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They teach you which holiday comes, things like that. We also had that. And that's also a part of the school-- with the school, through the government paid for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the teachers Jewish or non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one that I mentioned now, was a Jewish teacher, but no. They were Christian-- they were not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a young girl, what was the treatment of the non-Jews towards the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you, in Czechoslovakia, I didn't feel that. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] No. We lived together. We had neighbors-- non-Jewish neighbors. A lot of them were living with us, you know, and we-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --I didn't have that feeling-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --that they didn't like the Jews or something like that. Not in Czechoslovakia, no. It was wonderful people-- good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the population of Jews versus non-Jews in Munkacs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know Jews were 20,000-- 20,000 Jews. But the other in the population, I don't remember exactly. It's probably more, because it's-- mind you, Munkacs was a very Jewish place. There was a lot of Jews-- 20,000 Jews-- that's, I mean-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and, you know, and most people were religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jews live in one area, or were they spread out through the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were spread. We didn't have a ghetto, no. We were like neighbors who wasn't Jewish. Then we had maybe, in the other, and there was a lot of non-Jewish in our street. There was a lot of non-Jewish people, too. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know. We kept together-- kept together, even though we were very Orthodox. But it didn't make a difference. I had friends who weren't Jewish. They used to come in my house. I used to go to their house, I don't think I knew that I'm not allowed to eat there, except the fruit. But they didn't mind that. We used to play together-- I mean, we were younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of activities did you like to do with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to skate, we used to, in the wintertime, We used to do all kind of things for children-- doing jumping, doing, you know-- I mean, that was when I was younger. And after that, oh my god, after that, you know, it was like, we were very busy with school. We had a whole day school. There was no time in middle of the week to much of a friendship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any boarders or anybody living in your house? [SHAKES HEAD]

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody other than your family who lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had one worker who lived with us. He was the head of the business there. He used to live with us, also. But he had-- we had like a summer kitchen, and most of the time he was there. Because we didn't-- for him, we didn't have a place like-- my father wouldn't want a young boy-- he didn't want we should, you know-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim. But the other name, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your relatives live-- your grandparents, your aunts, your uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they lived in a different place. It wasn't far from Munkacs. Like my-- my grandfather one of them, he lived about-- was it two hours, or something like that, from us. Then my other grandfather, he lived further. Was already I don't know-- five hours to go there, with train. Since we didn't have a car.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you get to see your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my grandparents were there very often. They used to come to us, too, and I used to go there sometime in the summer, when I had time. When I was very young, I used to go for longer, and then, you know, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them, about your grandparents-- what memories you had of being with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very nice people and very-- very close. And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Very close family. Just memories, like-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] my grandmother used to cook special things for us, what we didn't have at home. You know, she made-- Used to tell us stories, but I don't remember the stories. And that was when I was younger, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you grandparents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One Meier, Hannah. That was from my father's side. Mother's was [NON-ENGLISH] and Nusen-- matter of fact, I knew my grandfather, Nusen [NON-ENGLISH], but they say no, because they find those papers what he wrote, and that's-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --the word-- no. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Nusen-- I don't know [MUMBLING]. [COUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Grandpa Meier and Grandma Hannah's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last name for Meier? [COUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: For Meier?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. [COUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Klein. I told you, because he was Polish, and they wouldn't-- they wouldn't-- they were married just in Jewish. They weren't-- because he was Polish and she wasn't Polish, and they were afraid. [SNEEZE] I don't know. They always-- the Polish people had a hard time of it, you know. So that's how she remained, and they took him away, because he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the name that they used in Czechoslovakia, the last name that they used? OK, we were talking about your grandfather's name, his last name. What was it when they lived in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Klein. That's it. And my grandmother was Steinberg, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he change to the name of Steinberg, or did he continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: He never changed it, no. He never changed it because he couldn't. They weren't married in a civil ceremony. They married just in Jewish. And she remained her name and he remained his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: These--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's for the reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father took the name of--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father took the name of my grandfather. That's why we are Steinberg. Otherwise we would be Klein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about how things changed for you in 1938 and '39, when Hungary came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. That was a big change for us, for everybody. First of all, my schooling was changed. Had to start over. I didn't know Hungarian, one word. And here I didn't want to go to first grade. I mean, I knew other things, but, you know, like it was-- whole thing, it was a change. And also, at home was a big change.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took away my father's [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] permanent. Then he couldn't work. What do you call it? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] License. They took away my father's license once they came in. And now the business were bad. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they had to go to one of-- they had went in another name, like there was a man who used to come and used  to use our machines, because he was also in that field, but he didn't have those machines. He used to come to us. My father used to let him. After that, when it happened, that, it went on his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name was Morichek. And it was also hard, because he couldn't import it anymore, because it was cut off from the Russian-- cut off from Poland. Budapest was here and there, but it wasn't business. There was no business. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And also the Jews had problems all over, already, that time. It was in Germany, and there was in Poland that time already, they had problems there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the problems that were occurring in other parts in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was aware, because we had, you know, like everything, like-- we had-- we were very well off, you know, before. And my father always used to say, you know, in Germany, they're standing in line for a potato. You don't have. You better get used to, also, we're going to have less. I remember my father said that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, my grandfather they took away in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Polish. Nothing happened. He was just Polish. One Saturday, they came in the morning and took him away. And they took him to Kamianets-Podilskyi, and they killed him. So my father knew there was a lot of problems, also used to cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear about your grandfather being taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandfather was alive-- my grandmother told us that they came and they took him away. Matter of fact, my grandmother came to us because she was shaken up terrible. And we never saw him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people knew. They said they killed him, and it was true. They took them-- they took all the Polish around, when they were like, you know, they took them there, and they knew where that is. They took him to Kamianets-Podilskyi, and they shot him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find that information out?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean when I find out?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you-- when were you told that he was shot at Kamianets-Podilskyi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, right away. I mean, my father used to say, used to cry, and that they killed him, his father. So we knew. We knew about it. But also, they didn't get to that, and that was in 1943, the Hungarians, when they took away my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- I told you, the one boy came in. He said, he doesn't have where to go. He has no home. And my father was a person who he took anybody in. You don't have a home? You come to us, stay with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he didn't stay in our house. He stayed up-- you know, there was a place on top of the house, but it was closed in. Like you could sleep there. He had a bed there. And my father actually didn't know who he is, and we didn't know he should-- he should be between us. And we were also religious, you know. He didn't know who that boy is. There was girls there in the house, you know. He didn't want that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the boy's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I have to think-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] Forgot. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Popolov-- Polovsky. The other name-- [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Popolowicz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Anyway. Tell me about how he had come to your father, what he was doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just came in the house. He just came in and he said-- well, it wasn't closed like here, the houses are closed where you can't get in. It was an open door. Anybody could-- it wasn't like-- you know, we didn't close doors in the day time. Just in the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he just walked in one afternoon. He came in and he said-- and then I guess we ate lunch. And he came in and he said that he hasn't got where to go, and yeah, he started-- he started to talk about something, Poland. Something about Holocaust things.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't-- we didn't even listen to him. We didn't realize things like that, especially young girls. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father said, he knows that in Poland it's not that they're taking-- to gather the Jews, and they have problems. But that's it. And-- well, he didn't really-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --he stayed there because we didn't want he should go around, walk around, and unless he had to go to the bathroom. That's it. Here, my father said, till he find out exactly what he is, but never happened, because it was Yom Kippur, and he was there. We didn't give him to eat. I don't know if he was religious or not. And my father said, let him come down. Let him eat with us. And just, we were there sitting down and eating something-- not finished-- and three cops came in. And they took that boy, and also my father. But my father, they took around in the house-- took him also up there where that boy was. After that, they came down, and they took my father away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He was locked up for a year. They let him out-- well, the business was bad. We got very poor, you know, because of that. And the Hungarian was-- there were very bad people. What can I tell you? Did you ever find out who the boy was and why they came to take him away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never find out, because they took away my father. And my mother was very heartbroken, the whole family, and what happened to him-- they probably locked him up, or they killed him or something like that. They just came and grabbed him and tied his hands and they took him away, the same time my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in [NON-ENGLISH] torture-- under the Hungarians. It was a place where they locked up people who did something. He didn't steal anything. He didn't, but that was-- that was it. And they didn't really work there. They just tortured him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After that, they decided to let him out, but there was already close to take away us, too. Did you have contact with your father during the time that he was taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We sent him sometime a package. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That, they let, send a package-- you know, food, something. But that's it. You couldn't go there and talk to him, or see him. They didn't let you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he wanted to send a package, so they gave him less to eat. They  had to give him less to eat. That's why they send the package. I mean, what kind of food he had there? It was terrible. The whole family was--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SHAKES HEAD] You run out of life, and that's it. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGH] Let's stop now and go to the next tape.
